FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018 (3.0 release), 10,755 children aged 9-10 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data using multiple linear regression.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in The Journal of Pediatrics (2022). No evidence of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social networking, texting, and video chat are analyzed as DVs (hours/day) with sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, and parental education as IVs. Although SM/phone-specific variables (social networking, texting, video chat) appear with their own regression coefficients, the other side of every analysis is entirely demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, country of birth). No individual-trait-level variable (behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, etc.) appears on the other side in any analysis. Fails the other-side rule.

notes: All analyses pair screen time modalities (including SM/phone) with demographic predictors only. No individual-trait outcome on the other side of any analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, December 2020 COVID-19 rapid-response survey, 4,106 adolescents ages 12-14 from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression on ABCD COVID-19 rapid-response data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Preventive Medicine Reports (2022). No evidence of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Checking COVID-related social media activity (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat) is measured as IV with its own adjusted risk ratio (ARR 1.09, 95% CI 1.00-1.19, p=0.049) predicting always-masking behavior. Masking behavior is a health behavior (individual-trait-level). Social media variable is SM-specific (named platforms listed). Other-side criterion met: masking behavior is a health/behavioral outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: ARR = 1.09 (95% CI 1.00-1.19, p=0.049) reported for social media frequency -> always masking in adjusted Poisson regression model (Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential Poisson regression analysis with numeric risk ratio reported for the SM-masking relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design. Not applicable.
notes: Social media exposure as IV predicting masking behavior (health behavior). Clear SM-specific variable with individual-trait outcome on the other side.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 follow-up 2018-2020 (4.0 release), N=8,753 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 2 data using multiple linear regression.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Pediatric Research (2022). No evidence of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Three DVs analyzed: VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). Per binding clarification, SMAQ and MPIQ are SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes on the other side. The IVs are entirely demographic (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parental education, parent marital status). Every analysis pairs SM/phone variables (SMAQ, MPIQ as DVs) with demographic-only predictors. The other side of every analysis is demographic-only. Fails the other-side rule.

notes: All DVs (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) are SM/phone/gaming variables per binding clarifications. All IVs are demographic. No individual-trait outcome on the other side of any analysis.
